summary: Communist revolutionary and long-time dictator of Yugoslavia (1892-1980), who led the Yugoslav Partisans against Axis occupation in World War II, then ruled the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia as President for Life. Tito broke decisively with Joseph Stalin in 1948, triggering the Informbiro period and Yugoslavia's expulsion from Cominform, and instead pursued a nonaligned foreign policy, co-founding the Non-Aligned Movement with Gamal Abdel Nasser, Jawaharlal Nehru, and Kwame Nkrumah and serving as its first Secretary-General.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How Tito is identified in giveaway lines: the Communist leader of the Yugoslav Partisans during World War II who went on to rule Yugoslavia as a socialist dictator until his death in 1980, keeping the country out of the Warsaw Pact.

clues.clue: Led the Yugoslav Partisans in World War II, then ruled Yugoslavia as Communist dictator/President for Life until his death in 1980
examples: name this head of the Yugoslav Partisans and long time leader of Yugoslavia.
For 10 points, name this Communist leader of Yugoslavia until his death in 1980.

clues.clue: Kept his country out of the Warsaw Pact, pursuing an independent socialist foreign policy
examples: Name this founding member of the Non-Aligned Movement, a longtime leader of Yugoslavia who kept his country out of the Warsaw Pact.
Stalin accused Rajk and other communists of following the doctrine of this Yugoslav leader, who resisted Soviet influence and never joined the Warsaw Pact.

clues.clue: Some conspiracy theorists have claimed the real Tito died and was replaced by a native Russian speaker, since mainstream scholarship holds this founder of the Yugoslav Partisans was actually Croatian
examples: This other Communist was suspected to have been replaced by a native speaker of Russian due to his unique accent. Mainstream scholarly opinion holds that this founder of the Yugoslav Partisans was actually Croatian.

works.name: World War II & the Yugoslav Partisans
works.description: As leader of the Communist-led Yugoslav Partisans, Tito fought both Axis occupiers and the rival royalist Chetnik movement, surviving several German attempts to kill or capture him and eventually merging his provisional government with that of exiled King Peter II.

clues.clue: Formed the Main Operational Group to win the Fall Weiss (Case White) offensive, a major German-led effort to destroy the Partisans
examples: This man formed the Main Operational Group to win the Fall Weiss offensive.

clues.clue: Was the target of Operation Knight's Move and defeated a much larger force at the Battle of the Neretva ("Battle of the Wounded") by blowing up all the bridges over the Neretva River
examples: This target of Operation Knight's Move successfully defeated a much larger army by blowing up all the bridges over the Neretva River in the so-called "Battle of the Wounded."

clues.clue: Merged his Partisan government with the government-in-exile of King Peter II via the Treaty of Vis, an agreement that aided his rise to power
examples: This man merged his government with Peter II in the Treaty of Vis.
This leader's rise to power was aided by an agreement with a monarch signed at Vis.

clues.clue: Put rival royalist Chetnik leader Draza Mihailovic on trial after the war, having earlier fought Mihailovic's forces for control of the town of Uzice (launching what he called the "leftist errors" there)
examples: This man put Draza Mihailovic on trial for leading the royalist Chetnik movement.
This leader launched the "leftist errors" while fighting for control of Uzice against Mihailovic's Chetniks.

clues.clue: Survived a German attempt to capture him in the Raid on Drvar, carried out by commando Otto Skorzeny as part of Operation Knight's Leap (Rosselsprung)
examples: As part of Operation Knight's Leap, Skorzeny also attempted to incapacitate this leader of the Yugoslav Partisans.

clues.clue: Terence Atherton's expedition mysteriously disappeared after contacting his Partisan forces during Operation Hydra
examples: Terence Atherton's expedition mysteriously disappeared after contacting this leader's forces during Operation Hydra.

works.name: Break with Stalin & the Informbiro Period
works.description: In 1948, Tito's Yugoslavia broke decisively with Joseph Stalin's Soviet Union, sparking the seven-year Informbiro period. Yugoslavia was expelled from Cominform, alleged Titoist sympathizers were purged and executed elsewhere in the Eastern Bloc, and Tito imprisoned domestic opponents at the Goli Otok camp.

clues.clue: The split (a distinct answerline, "Josip Broz Tito-Joseph Vissarionovich Stalin split") was formalized by a 1948 Cominform resolution accusing the Yugoslav Communist Party of splitting from Marxist-Leninism and expelling Yugoslavia from that body
examples: This event was made official with a 1948 resolution that accused another country's Communist party of splitting with Marxist-Leninism and expelled it from Cominform.
The official collapse of Yugoslav-Soviet relations came about with the expulsion of Yugoslavia from this pre-Warsaw Pact body, an alliance of Marxist-Leninist states formed in 1947.

clues.clue: The split sparked the Informbiro period, during which Tito imprisoned political opponents at the Goli Otok camp
examples: This event was followed by the Informbiro period in one of the relevant countries.
During his time in power, this leader's country adopted the motto "Brotherhood and Unity." Political opponents of this leader were sent to the prison island of Goli Otok during the Informbiro period.

clues.clue: Grew angry with Stalin over Stalin's impolitic involvement in the Greek Civil War and Tito's own foiled ambitions to annex the city of Trieste and unite Yugoslavia with Bulgaria (via the Lake Bled accord)
examples: One man in this event became angry at the other for that man's impolitic involvement in the Greek Civil War and attempts to incorporate Trieste into his country.
This man's falling out with a political ally foiled his plans to annex Trieste.

clues.clue: Reportedly wrote to Stalin, "Stop sending people to kill me... If you don't stop sending killers, I'll send a very fast working one to Moscow"
examples: This co-founder of the Non-Aligned Movement declared, "Stop sending people to kill me," after feuding with Joseph Stalin.
That letter came from this leader of the Yugoslav Partisans in the Second World War, who became Prime Minister and then President for Life in the post-war years.

clues.clue: Elsewhere in the Eastern Bloc, alleged sympathizers with his "doctrine" (Titoism) were purged and executed, including Hungary's Laszlo Rajk and Bulgaria's Traicho Kostov
examples: Supporters of one side were sent to the prison island Goli otok, and Traicho Kostov and Laszlo Rajk were both executed for their alleged support of the other.
Stalin accused Rajk and other communists of following the doctrine of this Yugoslav leader, who resisted Soviet influence and never joined the Warsaw Pact.

works.name: Founding the Non-Aligned Movement
works.description: Having broken with Moscow, Tito pursued a foreign policy of nonalignment, co-founding the Non-Aligned Movement alongside Gamal Abdel Nasser, Jawaharlal Nehru, and Kwame Nkrumah in the wake of the 1955 Bandung Conference and serving as its first Secretary-General from 1961.

clues.clue: Co-founded the Non-Aligned Movement with Nasser, Nehru, and Nkrumah, following the Bandung Conference
examples: With Nasser, Nehru, and Nkrumah, this man founded the Non-Aligned Movement following the Bandung Conference.

clues.clue: Was a co-founder of the Non-Aligned Movement and served as its first Secretary-General (from 1961), the only European among its founders, championing "independent roads to socialism"
examples: This man helped co-found and was the first Secretary-General of the Non-Aligned Movement, and his support of "independent roads to socialism" brought him in conflict with Stalin.
Name this man, the only European co-founder of the Non-Aligned Movement, which he became the first Secretary-General of in 1961.

works.name: Leadership of Socialist Yugoslavia
works.description: As leader of the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia, Tito founded the OZNA state security agency, promoted the unifying motto "Brotherhood and Unity" among Yugoslavia's constituent peoples, and clashed with the Catholic Church over his prosecution of Archbishop Aloysius Stepinac.

clues.clue: Founded the OZNA security agency, which arrested a Borba newspaper editor for criticizing the theory of workers' self-management
examples: This founder of the OZNA security agency ordered the arrest of a Borba newspaper editor for criticizing workers' self-management theory.

clues.clue: Promoted the motto "Brotherhood and Unity" among Yugoslavia's constituent peoples
examples: During his time in power, this leader's country adopted the motto "Brotherhood and Unity."

clues.clue: Was excommunicated by Pope Pius XII after convicting Archbishop Aloysius Stepinac of collaborating with the fascist Ustase regime
examples: Pius XII excommunicated this leader for convicting Archbishop Aloysius Stepinac of collaborating with the Ustase.

works.name: Legacy & Death
works.description: Tito's death in 1980 removed the unifying figure who had held Yugoslavia's constituent republics together; the country broke apart roughly a decade later, and censorship-defying "Goli Otok literature" about the earlier Informbiro-era prison camp flourished after his death.

clues.clue: Died in 1980; his death allowed the flourishing of "Goli Otok literature," dating back to the earlier Informbiro period
examples: This leader's death allowed the flourishing of "Goli Otok literature" dating back to the Informbiro period.

comprehensive_summary: Josip Broz Tito rose to power as leader of the Communist-led Yugoslav Partisans during World War II, forming the Main Operational Group to survive the Fall Weiss offensive, defeating a much larger German-led force at the Battle of the Neretva by destroying its bridges, surviving Otto Skorzeny's Raid on Drvar, and eventually merging his provisional government with that of the exiled King Peter II through the Treaty of Vis. He defeated the rival royalist Chetnik movement, fighting its leader Draza Mihailovic for control of Uzice and later trying him for collaboration. As leader of the postwar Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia, Tito founded the OZNA security service, promoted the motto "Brotherhood and Unity," and clashed with the Catholic Church, earning excommunication from Pius XII after convicting Archbishop Aloysius Stepinac of collaborating with the fascist Ustase. In 1948, Tito broke decisively with Joseph Stalin over disputes including Stalin's meddling in the Greek Civil War and Tito's own thwarted ambitions to annex Trieste and unite with Bulgaria; the split, formalized by Yugoslavia's expulsion from Cominform, triggered the Informbiro period, during which Tito imprisoned domestic opponents at the Goli Otok camp while alleged "Titoist" sympathizers like Hungary's Laszlo Rajk and Bulgaria's Traicho Kostov were purged elsewhere in the Eastern Bloc. Rather than rejoin the Soviet fold, Tito pursued a policy of nonalignment, co-founding the Non-Aligned Movement with Gamal Abdel Nasser, Jawaharlal Nehru, and Kwame Nkrumah after the 1955 Bandung Conference and serving as its first Secretary-General from 1961 as the movement's only European founder. He led Yugoslavia as President for Life, keeping it out of the Warsaw Pact, until his death in 1980, after which "Goli Otok literature" exposing the earlier prison camp began to flourish and Yugoslavia itself broke apart roughly a decade later.
recursive_suggestions: Battle of the Neretva — appeared as its own answerline; detailed content on the bridge demolitions and the Partisans' escape from encirclement

Josip Broz Tito-Joseph Vissarionovich Stalin split / Informbiro period — appeared as its own answerline with substantial detail on Cominform expulsion, Goli Otok, and purges of alleged Titoists abroad
Non-Aligned Movement — recurring co-topic (founded with Nasser, Nehru, Nkrumah at Bandung); would support its own standalone page
Aloysius Stepinac — appeared as his own bonus answerline; detailed content on his trial, imprisonment, and later beatification
Otto Skorzeny — recurring figure (Raid on Drvar, Operation Knight's Leap) with independent detail (rescue of Mussolini, kidnapping of Miklos Horthy Jr.) warranting his own page
Draza Mihailovic / Chetniks — Tito's rival WWII resistance movement, appeared as its own bonus answerline
Jozef Tiso — a different figure (Nazi puppet President of Slovakia) whose name is easily confused with Tito; one fetched tossup was entirely about Tiso and was excluded from this page as off-topic
